text:
Academic Letters: A Contribution in Honour of the Late Professor Johan A Heyns (1928–1994) This contribution is written as a letter to the late Professor Johan Adam Heyns who served Stellenbosch University as a senior lecturer in New Testament from 1966 to 1970. Written as one of a series of academic letters to deceased colleagues this genre provides a unique space for engagement with a particular colleague’s intellectual legacy at a particular time in history. This contribution reflects on Heyns’s early scholarship, which focused largely on apologetics and ethics, in the light of the growing reality of apartheid as a political system and the challenges it entailed within a context in which secularism was advancing as a major intellectual force.

Title: Looking back into the glass: The various phases of Paul Hendrikus Heyns’s academic career with the aid of a sixfold mirrorThe title encapsulates the different aspects that Paul Hendrikus Heyns’ academic career can be viewed from, depending on the tension between them. It is demonstrated that Heyns’ career had different phases or aspects depending on whether one looked at the relationship between traditional and progressive thinking, the struggle between the philosophies of Henk Stoker and that of the traditional Reformed thinking of Herman Bavinck as exemplified in the Theology of the Reformed Churches, or the struggle of the various aspects pertaining to apartheid. Reflection is made on the Stellenbosch Heyns in the light of the maturity he would later experience in Pretoria. It is shown that an academic career has its youthfulness in terms of academic endeavour with all the challenges that it poses, a stage in which confidence in one’s intellectual creativity is very much alive, the challenge to go to the frontiers of academic scholarship and one’s exposure to controversy in one’s scholarly endeavour. There is a stage of great academic insight and penetration but at the cost of some rigidity, a stage in which great academic creativity has already been fulfilled and therefore a certain calmness has set in, with less willingness to experiment. An appraisal of Paul Hendrikus Heyns’ contribution to Reformed thinking in South Africa is therefore possible.
